intent: Check whether the candidate can define routing in Express as the mapping between HTTP method, path, and handler, and can show the basic route declaration shape.
primaryQuestion: What is routing in Express, and how do you define a route?

expectedSignals.label: Defines routing as deciding how an application responds to a client request for a specific HTTP method and URL path.
Shows the basic shape app.METHOD(path, handler), such as app.get('/users', (req, res) => res.json(...)).
Explains that a route is matched by both path and HTTP method, not by path alone.
Mentions that handlers receive req and res, and may also receive next when control should be passed onward.
Understands that handlers should either send a response or pass control so the request does not hang.

redFlags: Says routing is only creating URLs without mentioning HTTP methods or handlers.
Cannot write a minimal app.get or router.get example.
Confuses Express routing with frontend client-side routing.
Believes a route handler automatically returns a response without using res or returning control.

scoringHints.description: Gives a precise method plus path plus handler definition, demonstrates a short route, and explains response or next control flow.
Can define and write a route but misses one nuance such as method matching or hanging requests.
Recognizes app.get but cannot explain what Express matches or what the handler must do.
Answer does not show basic Express routing knowledge.

followUps.question: Can you write a tiny GET /health route and explain which part is the method, path, and handler?
followUps.purpose: Force the answer into a concrete Express route declaration.

followUps.question: If a request is POST /users but only GET /users is defined, will that GET route run?
followUps.purpose: Verify that route matching includes the HTTP method.

followUps.question: What happens if your handler neither sends a response nor calls next()?

en.title: Express routing basics
en.displayQuestion: What is routing in Express, and how do you define a route?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands a route as an HTTP method, path, and handler mapping.
guidance: Define routing before jumping into code.
Use a concrete app.get('/health', handler) or router.get('/users', handler) example.
Explain that Express matches both the HTTP method and the route path.
Mention that the handler uses req and res, and may call next when another handler should continue.
commonMistakes: Describing routing as only URL naming.
Forgetting that POST /users and GET /users are different routes.
Confusing Express routing with React Router or browser navigation.
Leaving the request open by not sending a response or calling next.

vi.title: Nền tảng routing trong Express
vi.displayQuestion: Routing trong Express là gì và bạn định nghĩa route như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có hiểu route là mapping giữa HTTP method, path và handler hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa routing trước khi đi vào code.
Dùng ví dụ cụ thể như app.get('/health', handler) hoặc router.get('/users', handler).
Giải thích Express match cả HTTP method lẫn route path.
Nhắc rằng handler dùng req và res, và có thể gọi next khi cần chuyển tiếp xử lý.
commonMistakes: Mô tả routing chỉ là đặt tên URL.
Quên rằng POST /users và GET /users là hai route khác nhau.
Nhầm Express routing với React Router hoặc điều hướng trên browser.
Làm request bị treo vì không gửi response và cũng không gọi next.

ja.title: Express routing の基礎
ja.displayQuestion: Express における routing とは何で、route はどのように定義しますか。
ja.displayIntent: route を HTTP method、path、handler の対応として理解しているかを確認する。
guidance: コードを書く前に routing の意味を定義する。
app.get('/health', handler) や router.get('/users', handler) のような具体例を使う。
Express は HTTP method と route path の両方でマッチすることを説明する。
handler は req と res を使い、必要なら next で次の処理に渡すことに触れる。
commonMistakes: routing を URL の命名だけとして説明する。
POST /users と GET /users が別 route であることを忘れる。
Express routing と React Router やブラウザ遷移を混同する。
response を送らず next も呼ばず request を止めてしまう。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Routing
sourceReferences.note: Used for route definition, method/path matching, route handlers, and response lifecycle basics.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: app.METHOD
sourceReferences.note: Used for app.METHOD callback formats and route matching behavior.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can distinguish common Express HTTP method helpers and connect each method to normal REST intent and idempotency expectations.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between app.get(), app.post(), app.put(), app.patch(), and app.delete()?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that these helpers register handlers for different HTTP verbs on a path.
Connects GET to reading data and avoiding state changes.
Connects POST to creating resources or triggering non-idempotent actions.
Connects PUT to full replacement or idempotent upsert-style updates depending on the API contract.
Connects PATCH to partial update and DELETE to deletion or deactivation.
Mentions that Express does not enforce REST semantics; the application design and handler logic must enforce them.

redFlags: Says these methods are interchangeable because they can all return JSON.
Uses GET for destructive or state-changing actions without recognizing the risk.
Cannot explain the practical difference between PUT and PATCH.
Believes Express automatically creates database records when app.post is used.

scoringHints.description: Correctly maps each method to common REST semantics, explains idempotency at a practical level, and notes that Express only routes by method.
Understands the main method purposes but is shallow on PUT versus PATCH or REST semantics enforcement.
Knows method names but treats them mostly as naming conventions.
Answer is incorrect about HTTP method behavior or Express method routing.

followUps.question: When would you choose PATCH /users/:id instead of PUT /users/:id?
followUps.purpose: Check partial versus full update semantics.

followUps.question: Why is GET /delete-user?id=1 a poor API design?
followUps.purpose: Probe safe method semantics and accidental side effects.

followUps.question: Does Express itself stop you from changing data inside app.get()?
followUps.purpose: Verify separation between framework routing and API contract discipline.

en.title: HTTP method route helpers
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between app.get(), app.post(), app.put(), app.patch(), and app.delete()?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can explain route helpers through HTTP method semantics, not just syntax.
guidance: Start by saying each helper registers a route for one HTTP verb.
Map GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE to common REST use cases.
Explain PUT versus PATCH carefully because this is a common interview gap.
Clarify that Express routes by method but does not enforce business semantics.
commonMistakes: Treating all method helpers as interchangeable.
Using GET for actions that mutate data.
Saying PUT and PATCH are exactly the same.
Assuming Express automatically implements CRUD behavior.

vi.title: Các helper HTTP method
vi.displayQuestion: Sự khác biệt giữa app.get(), app.post(), app.put(), app.patch(), app.delete() là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có giải thích method helper dựa trên ý nghĩa HTTP method, không chỉ cú pháp.
guidance: Bắt đầu bằng việc nói mỗi helper đăng ký route cho một HTTP verb.
Map GET, POST, PUT, PATCH và DELETE với các use case REST thường gặp.
Giải thích kỹ PUT và PATCH vì đây là điểm ứng viên hay nhầm.
Làm rõ Express chỉ route theo method, không tự enforce semantic nghiệp vụ.
commonMistakes: Xem mọi method helper là tương đương nhau.
Dùng GET cho hành động làm thay đổi dữ liệu.
Nói PUT và PATCH giống hệt nhau.
Tưởng Express tự implement CRUD khi dùng các method này.

ja.title: HTTP method route helper
ja.displayQuestion: app.get(), app.post(), app.put(), app.patch(), app.delete() の違いは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: method helper を単なる構文ではなく HTTP method の意味で説明できるかを確認する。
guidance: 各 helper は特定の HTTP verb に対する route を登録するものだと説明する。
GET、POST、PUT、PATCH、DELETE を一般的な REST の用途に対応づける。
PUT と PATCH の違いは面接でよく曖昧になるため丁寧に説明する。
Express は method で route するだけで、業務上の semantic は自動では enforce しないと明確にする。
commonMistakes: すべての method helper を同じものとして扱う。
データを変更する処理に GET を使う。
PUT と PATCH が完全に同じだと言う。
Express が CRUD 動作を自動実装すると考える。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Route methods
sourceReferences.note: Used for method-based route helpers and app.all behavior.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: app.METHOD
sourceReferences.note: Used for callback and method-specific route registration details.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands the three core handler parameters and the request-response control flow in Express.
primaryQuestion: What are req, res, and next in an Express route handler?

expectedSignals.label: Explains req as the request object containing path params, query, body, headers, cookies, method, URL, and other request data.
Explains res as the response object used to set status, headers, cookies, and send JSON, text, files, redirects, or end the response.
Explains next as the function that passes control to the next matching middleware or route handler.
Mentions next(err) for error handling and next('route') for skipping remaining callbacks in the current route when appropriate.
Understands that once a final response is sent, the handler should not continue into another response path.

redFlags: Thinks req and res are plain custom objects created by the developer.
Cannot name common data sources on req such as params, query, body, or headers.
Calls next after sending a normal response without understanding duplicate response risk.
Does not know how errors are forwarded to Express error-handling middleware.

scoringHints.description: Clearly explains req, res, next, normal flow, error flow, and response completion risks with examples.
Correctly defines the three parameters but misses error flow or double-send nuance.
Knows names but cannot explain how they control the request lifecycle.
Answer does not demonstrate practical Express handler understanding.

followUps.question: Where would you read an Authorization header, a route id, and a JSON payload from req?
What can go wrong if you call res.json() and then call next() into another response handler?
followUps.purpose: Check duplicate response and headers-sent reasoning.

followUps.question: How do you pass an error from a route handler to centralized error middleware?
followUps.purpose: Check next(err) and error middleware awareness.

en.title: req, res, and next
en.displayQuestion: What are req, res, and next in an Express route handler?
en.displayIntent: Check understanding of the request object, response object, and middleware control flow.
guidance: Explain req as incoming request data and metadata.
Explain res as the object used to build and send the response.
Explain next as the control handoff to the next middleware or error handler.
Mention that a handler should avoid sending more than one final response.
commonMistakes: Only saying req is input and res is output without details.
Forgetting next(err) for centralized errors.
Calling next after sending a response without a reason.
Mixing up req.params, req.query, and req.body.

vi.title: req, res và next
vi.displayQuestion: req, res, next trong route handler là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về request object, response object và control flow của middleware.
guidance: Giải thích req chứa dữ liệu và metadata của request đi vào.
Giải thích res dùng để dựng và gửi response.
Giải thích next là cơ chế chuyển quyền xử lý sang middleware hoặc error handler tiếp theo.
Nhắc rằng một handler không nên gửi final response nhiều lần.
commonMistakes: Chỉ nói req là input và res là output mà không có chi tiết.
Quên next(err) cho centralized error handling.
Gọi next sau khi đã gửi response mà không hiểu lý do.
Nhầm req.params, req.query và req.body.

ja.title: req、res、next
ja.displayQuestion: Express の route handler における req、res、next とは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: request object、response object、middleware control flow の理解を確認する。
guidance: req は受信 request のデータと metadata を持つものだと説明する。
res は response を構築して送信するための object だと説明する。
next は次の middleware または error handler に処理を渡す仕組みだと説明する。
1 つの handler で final response を複数回送らないようにする点に触れる。
commonMistakes: req は input、res は output とだけ言って詳細がない。
centralized error handling のための next(err) を忘れる。
response 送信後に理由なく next を呼ぶ。
req.params、req.query、req.body を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for req, res, next, middleware tasks, and hanging request behavior.

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Routing route handlers
sourceReferences.note: Used for multiple handlers and next('route') behavior.

intent: Verify whether the candidate can distinguish route parameters from query strings and access them correctly in Express.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between a route parameter like :id and a query string like ?id=1, and how do you access each?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that route parameters are named path segments defined in the route path, such as /users/:id.
Explains that route parameters are read from req.params, such as req.params.id.
Explains that query strings are key-value options after ?, not part of the route path.
Explains that query values are read from req.query, such as req.query.page or req.query.id.
Uses route params for resource identity and query strings for filtering, sorting, pagination, search, or optional modifiers.
Mentions that both params and query values come from the client and need parsing and validation.

redFlags: Reads /users/:id from req.query.id.
Thinks query strings change which route path Express matches.
Uses query strings for required resource identity without a clear reason.
Assumes req.params.id is already a number or already validated.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates path identity from query options, gives correct accessors, and mentions parsing and validation.
Knows req.params and req.query but misses API design guidance or validation.
Can access one source but confuses when to use each.
Cannot distinguish route params from query strings in Express.

followUps.question: For GET /users/42?includePosts=true&page=2, what are req.params and req.query?
Why would /users/:id be clearer than /users?id=42 for fetching one user?
followUps.purpose: Check resource identity and API design judgment.

followUps.question: Is req.params.id a number automatically? What should you do before using it in a database query?

en.title: Route params versus query strings
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between a route parameter like :id and a query string like ?id=1, and how do you access each?
en.displayIntent: Check correct use of req.params and req.query and the API design meaning behind each.
guidance: Use /users/:id and /users?role=admin&page=2 as examples.
Say route params come from named path segments and are read from req.params.
Say query strings are not part of the route path and are read from req.query.
Mention validation and type conversion for both sources.
commonMistakes: Reading path parameters from req.query.
Treating query strings as part of Express route matching.
Using query strings for required resource identity by habit.
Assuming user-controlled string values are safe to use directly.

vi.title: Route parameter và query string
vi.displayQuestion: Route parameter (:id) và query string (?id=1) khác nhau như thế nào? Truy cập bằng cách nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cách dùng đúng req.params, req.query và ý nghĩa thiết kế API phía sau.
guidance: Dùng ví dụ /users/:id và /users?role=admin&page=2.
Nói route param đến từ named path segment và đọc bằng req.params.
Nói query string không thuộc route path và đọc bằng req.query.
Nhắc validation và chuyển kiểu cho cả hai nguồn dữ liệu.
commonMistakes: Đọc path parameter từ req.query.
Tưởng query string tham gia vào route matching của Express.
Dùng query string cho resource identity bắt buộc theo thói quen.
Giả định giá trị string từ client là an toàn để dùng trực tiếp.

ja.title: route parameter と query string
ja.displayQuestion: route parameter (:id) と query string (?id=1) はどう違い、それぞれどのように取得しますか。
ja.displayIntent: req.params と req.query の正しい使い分けと API design 上の意味を確認する。
guidance: /users/:id と /users?role=admin&page=2 を例に使う。
route param は path の named segment から来て req.params で読むと説明する。
query string は route path の一部ではなく req.query で読むと説明する。
どちらも validation と type conversion が必要だと述べる。
commonMistakes: path parameter を req.query から読む。
query string が Express の route matching に含まれると考える。
必須の resource identity を習慣的に query string にする。
client 由来の string 値をそのまま安全に使えると思う。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Route parameters
sourceReferences.note: Used for req.params and route parameter behavior.

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Routing route paths
sourceReferences.note: Used for the note that query strings are not part of the route path.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: req.query
sourceReferences.note: Used for query string access and parser behavior.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands express.Router as a modular, mountable routing and middleware unit for organizing routes outside the root app.
primaryQuestion: What is express.Router(), and why use it instead of attaching all routes directly to app?

expectedSignals.label: Defines express.Router() as a modular, mountable router that can contain route handlers and middleware.
Explains that a router behaves like a mini application for routing and middleware, but is mounted into the main app.
Shows an example such as const usersRouter = express.Router(); usersRouter.get('/', ...); app.use('/users', usersRouter).
Connects Router to modular files, feature ownership, testability, and reducing a large app.ts or server.ts file.
Mentions router-level middleware for feature-specific behavior such as auth, logging, validation, or parameter preloading.

redFlags: Thinks Router starts a separate HTTP server.
Cannot explain how a router is mounted with app.use.
Says Router is only for performance or only for TypeScript.
Puts every route in one file and sees no maintainability issue as the codebase grows.

scoringHints.description: Defines Router accurately, demonstrates mounting, and explains modularity, ownership, middleware scoping, and testability benefits.
Knows Router groups routes but misses middleware scoping or mount behavior details.
Has used Router but cannot distinguish it from app or explain why it matters.
Answer does not show practical Router knowledge.

followUps.question: Can you sketch a users.routes.ts file and show how app.ts mounts it?
What kind of middleware would you attach to a usersRouter but not to the whole app?
Does express.Router() listen on a port by itself?
followUps.purpose: Correct confusion between router and server.

en.title: express.Router for modular routes
en.displayQuestion: What is express.Router(), and why use it instead of attaching all routes directly to app?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can use Router as a modular, mountable routing unit.
guidance: Define Router as a mountable route and middleware container.
Show app.use('/users', usersRouter) to explain how the prefix is applied.
Explain why feature route files are easier to maintain than one large app file.
Mention router-specific middleware and ownership boundaries.
commonMistakes: Thinking Router creates another server.
Forgetting that routers are mounted with app.use or another router.use.
Using Router only as a folder convention without middleware benefits.
Keeping all routes in app.ts as the project grows.

vi.title: express.Router cho route modular
vi.displayQuestion: express.Router() là gì và tại sao cần dùng nó thay vì gắn tất cả route vào app?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có biết dùng Router như một đơn vị routing có thể mount và tách module hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa Router là container cho route và middleware có thể mount.
Dùng app.use('/users', usersRouter) để giải thích prefix được áp dụng như thế nào.
Giải thích vì sao route theo feature dễ maintain hơn một file app lớn.
Nhắc middleware riêng của router và boundary ownership.
commonMistakes: Tưởng Router tạo thêm một server riêng.
Quên rằng router được mount bằng app.use hoặc router.use.
Dùng Router chỉ như convention folder mà không hiểu lợi ích middleware.
Giữ toàn bộ routes trong app.ts khi dự án lớn dần.

ja.title: modular routes のための express.Router
ja.displayQuestion: express.Router() とは何で、すべての route を app に直接付ける代わりに使う理由は何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: Router を mount 可能な modular routing unit として使えるかを確認する。
guidance: Router は route と middleware を入れられる mountable container だと定義する。
app.use('/users', usersRouter) で prefix がどのように適用されるか説明する。
feature ごとの route file が巨大な app file より maintain しやすい理由を説明する。
router-specific middleware と ownership boundary に触れる。
commonMistakes: Router が別の server を作ると思う。
router は app.use または router.use で mount することを忘れる。
Router を middleware の利点なしに folder convention としてだけ使う。
project が成長しても全 route を app.ts に置き続ける。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: express.Router
sourceReferences.note: Used for Router as a modular, mountable route handler and mini-app concept.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: Router
sourceReferences.note: Used for router middleware behavior and mounting.

intent: Check whether the candidate can group related routes under a common prefix using app.use and express.Router.
primaryQuestion: How do you group routes by prefix, for example /api/v1/users?

expectedSignals.label: Uses a router for the feature, such as usersRouter.get('/', ...) and usersRouter.get('/:id', ...).
Mounts the router at a prefix with app.use('/api/v1/users', usersRouter).
Understands that routes inside the router are relative to the mount path.
Explains that prefix grouping helps versioning, module ownership, middleware scoping, and avoiding repeated strings.
Mentions that nested routers can be used for sub-resources when it keeps route ownership clear.

redFlags: Repeats /api/v1/users manually on every route in multiple files without recognizing drift risk.
Mounts a router but still defines all internal paths with the full prefix by mistake.
Cannot explain why usersRouter.get('/:id') becomes /api/v1/users/:id after mounting.
Mixes API versioning into unrelated controller logic instead of route composition.

scoringHints.description: Shows correct prefix mounting, relative router paths, and connects grouping to versioning, middleware, ownership, and maintainability.
Can mount a router with a prefix but misses one design benefit or relative path detail.
Knows app.use exists but struggles to reason about final paths after mounting.
Cannot group Express routes by prefix correctly.

followUps.question: If usersRouter defines router.get('/:id'), what full URL does it handle after app.use('/api/v1/users', usersRouter)?
Where would you attach auth middleware if all /api/v1/users routes require authentication?
followUps.purpose: Check prefix-level or router-level middleware placement.

followUps.question: When would you create a separate v2 router instead of adding conditionals inside v1 handlers?

en.title: Grouping routes by prefix
en.displayQuestion: How do you group routes by prefix, for example /api/v1/users?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can mount feature routers under a shared API prefix.
guidance: Use a usersRouter with relative paths such as '/' and '/:id'.
Mount it with app.use('/api/v1/users', usersRouter).
Explain that the mount path and router path are composed into the final URL.
Discuss versioning and middleware scoping as reasons to group by prefix.
commonMistakes: Duplicating the full prefix on every route.
Defining full paths inside the router after already mounting a prefix.
Putting versioning decisions deep inside every handler.
Forgetting that app.use can mount routers as middleware.

vi.title: Group routes theo prefix
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để group routes theo prefix, ví dụ /api/v1/users?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có biết mount feature router dưới một API prefix chung hay không.
guidance: Dùng usersRouter với path tương đối như '/' và '/:id'.
Mount bằng app.use('/api/v1/users', usersRouter).
Giải thích mount path và router path được ghép thành URL cuối cùng.
Nói về versioning và middleware scoping như lý do group theo prefix.
commonMistakes: Lặp lại toàn bộ prefix ở mọi route.
Đã mount prefix nhưng bên trong router vẫn khai báo full path.
Đưa logic versioning vào sâu trong từng handler.
Quên app.use có thể mount router như middleware.

ja.title: prefix による route group
ja.displayQuestion: /api/v1/users のような prefix で routes を group するにはどうしますか。
ja.displayIntent: feature router を共通 API prefix の下に mount できるかを確認する。
guidance: usersRouter の中では '/' や '/:id' のような relative path を使う。
app.use('/api/v1/users', usersRouter) で mount する。
mount path と router path が合成されて最終 URL になることを説明する。
prefix grouping の理由として versioning と middleware scoping を説明する。
commonMistakes: すべての route に full prefix を重複して書く。
prefix で mount 済みなのに router 内でも full path を書く。
versioning の判断を各 handler の深い場所に入れる。
app.use で router を middleware として mount できることを忘れる。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: express.Router
sourceReferences.note: Used for mounting routers under a path prefix.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: app.use
sourceReferences.note: Used for mount path behavior and middleware path composition.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can distinguish app.use middleware mounting from app.get GET route registration.
primaryQuestion: How is app.use() different from app.get()?

expectedSignals.label: Explains app.get(path, handler) registers a route for HTTP GET requests to the path.
Explains app.use(path?, middleware) mounts middleware or routers for all HTTP methods when the base path matches.
Mentions that app.use('/api', middleware) also applies to subpaths such as /api/users.
Mentions that app.use without a path applies to every request reaching that point in the middleware stack.
Connects app.use to cross-cutting middleware, static files, body parsers, routers, and 404 handlers.

redFlags: Says app.use and app.get are the same except for naming.
Thinks app.use only handles GET requests.
Registers body parser or auth middleware after routes that need it and expects it to run retroactively.
Does not understand path prefix matching for app.use.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates middleware mounting from GET route registration, including all-method behavior, prefix matching, and order implications.
Understands the main difference but misses prefix matching or ordering detail.
Has used both APIs but cannot predict which requests they handle.
Answer is incorrect about app.use or app.get behavior.

followUps.question: Which requests will app.use('/api', mw) see: GET /api, POST /api/users, and GET /public?
followUps.purpose: Check method and prefix matching behavior.

followUps.question: Why does app.use(express.json()) need to be registered before routes that read req.body?
Give one example where app.use is correct and app.get would be wrong.

en.title: app.use versus app.get
en.displayQuestion: How is app.use() different from app.get()?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can separate middleware mounting from GET route handling.
guidance: Define app.get as a GET route registration method.
Define app.use as middleware or router mounting for matching base paths.
Mention that app.use can affect all HTTP methods and subpaths.
Tie the difference to body parsers, auth middleware, routers, static files, and 404 handling.
commonMistakes: Thinking app.use is just a generic version of app.get.
Forgetting that app.use can run for POST, PUT, DELETE, and other methods.
Missing prefix matching such as /api matching /api/users.
Registering middleware too late in the stack.

vi.title: app.use khác app.get
vi.displayQuestion: app.use() khác app.get() như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có phân biệt middleware mounting với route GET hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa app.get là cách đăng ký route cho HTTP GET.
Định nghĩa app.use là cách mount middleware hoặc router cho base path match.
Nhắc rằng app.use có thể áp dụng cho mọi HTTP method và subpath.
Liên hệ với body parser, auth middleware, router, static file và 404 handling.
commonMistakes: Tưởng app.use chỉ là phiên bản tổng quát của app.get.
Quên app.use có thể chạy cho POST, PUT, DELETE và các method khác.
Bỏ sót prefix matching như /api match /api/users.
Đăng ký middleware quá muộn trong stack.

ja.title: app.use と app.get の違い
ja.displayQuestion: app.use() と app.get() はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: middleware mounting と GET route handling を区別できるかを確認する。
guidance: app.get は HTTP GET route を登録する method だと定義する。
app.use は matching base path に middleware や router を mount するものだと定義する。
app.use はすべての HTTP method と subpath に適用され得ることを述べる。
body parser、auth middleware、router、static files、404 handling と関連づける。
commonMistakes: app.use を app.get の汎用版だと思う。
app.use が POST、PUT、DELETE などにも走ることを忘れる。
/api が /api/users にも match する prefix matching を見落とす。
middleware を stack の遅すぎる位置に登録する。

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: app.use
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware mounting, default path, prefix matching, and callback formats.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: app.get
sourceReferences.note: Used for GET route registration behavior.

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for application-level middleware and order examples.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands wildcard and optional route patterns, their use cases, and Express 4 versus Express 5 syntax differences.
primaryQuestion: When would you use route wildcards and optional parameters such as * or :id?

expectedSignals.label: Uses wildcard routes for catch-all behavior, SPA fallback, 404 handling, or path-prefix behavior when a broader match is intentionally needed.
Uses optional parameters when one handler can legitimately support both a route with and without a segment, while keeping the contract clear.
Explains that overly broad wildcard routes can shadow more specific routes if registered too early.
Mentions that query strings are often better than optional path segments for filters and optional modifiers.
Shows version awareness: Express 4 examples often use * or :id?, while Express 5 uses named wildcards and brace-style optional segments such as /*splat, /{*splat}, or /users{/:id}.
Mentions that wildcard captures and optional params should still be validated before use.

redFlags: Uses a wildcard as a substitute for designing clear routes.
Registers a catch-all route before feature routes and expects later routes to run.
Does not know that Express 5 changed handling of ?, *, and optional path syntax.
Uses optional path segments for many unrelated behaviors, making the route contract ambiguous.

scoringHints.description: Explains valid wildcard and optional segment use cases, order risks, query-string alternatives, validation, and Express 5 syntax changes.
Understands use cases and route order but is light on Express version differences or API design trade-offs.
Knows wildcard and optional params exist but cannot explain when they are appropriate or risky.
Answer would likely produce fragile or incorrect route matching.

followUps.question: When is ?page=1 clearer than making a path segment optional?
What happens if a catch-all route is registered before /api/users?
followUps.purpose: Verify route order and shadowing awareness.

followUps.question: How would you write a catch-all in Express 5 without relying on a bare * route?

en.title: Wildcards and optional params
en.displayQuestion: When would you use route wildcards and optional parameters such as * or :id?
en.displayIntent: Check route pattern judgment, catch-all ordering, and Express 5 syntax awareness.
guidance: Separate wildcard catch-all use cases from optional segment use cases.
Explain why broad matches should be registered late and tested carefully.
Mention that filters and pagination usually belong in query strings.
Call out Express 5 named wildcard and brace-style optional syntax instead of assuming Express 4 patterns always work.
commonMistakes: Using wildcard routes as a shortcut for unclear API design.
Putting catch-all routes before specific routes.
Assuming :id? and bare * behave the same across Express versions.
Making one handler handle too many unrelated URL shapes.

vi.title: Wildcard và optional parameter
vi.displayQuestion: Route wildcard (*) và optional parameter (:id?) dùng khi nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra judgment về route pattern, thứ tự catch-all và nhận biết thay đổi syntax ở Express 5.
guidance: Tách use case của wildcard catch-all khỏi optional segment.
Giải thích vì sao route match rộng nên đặt muộn và cần test kỹ.
Nhắc rằng filter và pagination thường phù hợp hơn với query string.
Nêu rõ Express 5 dùng named wildcard và brace-style optional syntax, không nên mặc định pattern Express 4 luôn chạy.
commonMistakes: Dùng wildcard route để né thiết kế API rõ ràng.
Đặt catch-all route trước route cụ thể.
Tưởng :id? và bare * hoạt động giống nhau ở mọi version Express.
Bắt một handler xử lý quá nhiều dạng URL không liên quan.

ja.title: wildcard と optional parameter
ja.displayQuestion: route wildcard (*) や optional parameter (:id?) はいつ使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: route pattern の判断、catch-all の順序、Express 5 syntax への理解を確認する。
guidance: wildcard catch-all の用途と optional segment の用途を分けて説明する。
広く match する route は後ろに置き、慎重に test すべき理由を説明する。
filter や pagination は通常 query string の方が自然だと述べる。
Express 5 では named wildcard と brace-style optional syntax を使う点に触れ、Express 4 pattern が常に動く前提にしない。
commonMistakes: 不明確な API design の代わりに wildcard route を使う。
catch-all route を具体的な route より前に置く。
:id? や bare * が Express の全 version で同じように動くと思う。
1 つの handler に関係の薄い URL shape を詰め込みすぎる。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Route paths
sourceReferences.note: Used for route path matching and warnings about ?, +, *, [], and () in Express 5.

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Moving to Express 5
sourceReferences.note: Used for named wildcards, optional parameter omissions, and path syntax changes.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: app.all
sourceReferences.note: Used for Express 5 catch-all examples using named splat patterns.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands Express routing and middleware as an ordered stack where earlier matching handlers can affect or terminate later matching handlers.
primaryQuestion: Does route declaration order matter in Express? Why?

expectedSignals.label: States that order matters because Express evaluates middleware and routes in the order they are registered.
Explains that a matching handler can send a response and terminate the request-response cycle before later routes run.
Explains that generic routes such as /users/:id or catch-all routes should usually come after more specific routes such as /users/me or /users/search.
Mentions middleware order: parsers and auth must be registered before routes that need them; error handlers and 404 handlers usually come after routes.
Can reason through next(), next('route'), and next('router') at a high level when multiple handlers match.

redFlags: Believes Express automatically chooses the most specific route regardless of registration order.
Registers catch-all or parameterized routes before static routes without recognizing shadowing risk.
Expects middleware registered after a route to affect that already-handled route.
Cannot diagnose why /users/me is being treated as id='me'.

scoringHints.description: Explains ordered stack execution, response termination, specific-before-generic route design, middleware placement, and next variants.
Understands order matters but misses one nuance such as middleware placement or next('route').
Has seen order issues but cannot predict matching behavior reliably.
Answer suggests unsafe assumptions about Express route matching.

followUps.question: If /users/:id is declared before /users/me, what may happen for GET /users/me?
Where should a 404 handler be registered relative to normal routes and error middleware?
Can you walk through logging middleware, auth middleware, route handler, 404 handler, and error handler in order?

en.title: Route declaration order
en.displayQuestion: Does route declaration order matter in Express? Why?
en.displayIntent: Check ordered middleware and route stack reasoning.
guidance: State clearly that Express uses registration order.
Explain how earlier handlers can send a response or pass control.
Use /users/me versus /users/:id as a concrete example.
Include middleware placement for parsers, auth, 404, and errors.
commonMistakes: Assuming Express always picks the most specific route.
Putting generic parameterized routes before static routes.
Adding middleware after routes and expecting it to affect earlier routes.
Putting a catch-all route too early.

vi.title: Thứ tự khai báo route
vi.displayQuestion: Thứ tự khai báo route có quan trọng không? Tại sao?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra khả năng reasoning về stack route và middleware có thứ tự.
guidance: Nói rõ Express chạy theo thứ tự đăng ký.
Giải thích handler phía trước có thể gửi response hoặc chuyển tiếp control.
Dùng ví dụ /users/me và /users/:id.
Bao gồm vị trí middleware cho parser, auth, 404 và error.
commonMistakes: Tưởng Express luôn chọn route cụ thể nhất.
Đặt route parameterized quá rộng trước route static.
Thêm middleware sau route rồi kỳ vọng nó ảnh hưởng route trước đó.
Đặt catch-all route quá sớm.

ja.title: route 宣言順序
ja.displayQuestion: Express で route の宣言順序は重要ですか。なぜですか。
ja.displayIntent: 順序を持つ middleware と route stack を理解しているか確認する。
guidance: Express は登録順に処理するとはっきり述べる。
前の handler が response を送るか control を渡すかで後続が変わることを説明する。
/users/me と /users/:id を具体例にする。
parser、auth、404、error middleware の配置も含める。
commonMistakes: Express が常に最も具体的な route を選ぶと思う。
広い parameterized route を static route より前に置く。
route の後に middleware を追加して前の route に効くと思う。
catch-all route を早すぎる位置に置く。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for registration order, next(), and request hanging behavior.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: router.use
sourceReferences.note: Used for router middleware order and precedence.

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Routing route handlers
sourceReferences.note: Used for multiple route handlers and next('route').

intent: Assess whether the candidate can implement a maintainable 404 fallback after all normal routes and before centralized error handling.
primaryQuestion: How do you handle a route that does not exist in Express?

expectedSignals.label: Adds a catch-all 404 middleware after all known routes have been registered.
Uses res.status(404).json(...) or res.sendStatus(404) with a consistent API error shape.
Understands that the 404 handler is for unmatched requests, not exceptions thrown by matched handlers.
Places centralized error-handling middleware after the 404 handler when using next(err) patterns.
Mentions that in Express 5 catch-all route patterns should use current syntax such as app.use((req, res) => ...) or a named splat pattern when a route path is needed.

redFlags: Places the 404 middleware before normal routes.
Handles 404 as a generic 500 error.
Throws an exception for every unmatched route instead of returning a clear 404 response.
Returns HTML for a JSON API without considering the API contract.

scoringHints.description: Places a clear 404 fallback after routes, distinguishes unmatched requests from errors, and keeps response shape consistent.
Knows to add a final 404 handler but is slightly vague about error middleware ordering or response shape.
Can return 404 in a specific handler but lacks a global fallback pattern.
Answer would not reliably handle unmatched routes.

followUps.question: Where exactly would you register the 404 handler relative to app.use('/api', apiRouter) and the error handler?
Why is an unmatched route usually a 404 rather than a 500?
What should the 404 response look like for a public JSON API?

en.title: Handling unmatched routes
en.displayQuestion: How do you handle a route that does not exist in Express?
en.displayIntent: Check correct 404 fallback placement and API error response design.
guidance: Register all normal routes first.
Add a final 404 middleware or catch-all route after normal routes.
Return a consistent status and JSON error body for APIs.
Explain that error-handling middleware is separate from unmatched-route handling.
commonMistakes: Placing the 404 handler before routes.
Treating every 404 as a server exception.
Returning inconsistent error shapes.
Forgetting Express 5 route pattern differences for catch-all syntax.

vi.title: Xử lý route không tồn tại
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để xử lý route không tồn tại (404)?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra vị trí đặt 404 fallback và thiết kế error response cho API.
guidance: Đăng ký toàn bộ route bình thường trước.
Thêm middleware hoặc catch-all 404 cuối cùng sau normal routes.
Trả status và JSON error body nhất quán cho API.
Giải thích error-handling middleware là phần riêng với unmatched-route handling.
commonMistakes: Đặt 404 handler trước các route.
Xem mọi 404 là server exception.
Trả error shape không nhất quán.
Quên khác biệt syntax catch-all route ở Express 5.

ja.title: 存在しない route の処理
ja.displayQuestion: Express で存在しない route、つまり 404 はどのように処理しますか。
ja.displayIntent: 404 fallback の正しい配置と API error response design を確認する。
guidance: 通常の route をすべて登録してから行う。
normal routes の後に final 404 middleware または catch-all route を追加する。
API では一貫した status と JSON error body を返す。
error-handling middleware と unmatched-route handling は別だと説明する。
commonMistakes: 404 handler を route より前に置く。
すべての 404 を server exception として扱う。
error shape が一貫しない。
Express 5 の catch-all syntax の違いを忘れる。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware stack ordering and request-response completion.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: res.status
sourceReferences.note: Used for setting status codes in responses.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: app.all
sourceReferences.note: Used for catch-all route patterns and global path logic examples.

intent: Verify whether the candidate understands app.all as path-specific logic that matches every HTTP method, and can choose it appropriately.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that app.all(path, callbacks) matches all HTTP methods for the given path.
Uses app.all for path-specific preconditions such as authentication, loading a resource, method-independent logging, or shared checks.
Understands callbacks do not have to be final endpoints; they can call next() to continue to later matching routes.
Distinguishes app.all from app.use: app.all is route-style method matching on a path, while app.use mounts middleware by base path and can match subpaths.
Mentions that app.all can be risky if used too broadly or registered before routes unintentionally.

redFlags: Says app.all means all paths instead of all HTTP methods for a path.
Uses app.all everywhere instead of clear method-specific routes.
Forgets to call next when using app.all as a precondition middleware.
Cannot distinguish app.all from app.use.

scoringHints.description: Defines all-method path matching, gives precondition examples, explains next flow, and distinguishes it from app.use.
Understands all-method matching but is light on app.use distinction or ordering risk.
Has seen app.all but cannot explain when it should be used.
Answer is factually wrong about app.all.

followUps.question: Would app.all('/secret', auth) run for DELETE /secret? What about GET /public?
When would app.use('/api', auth) be more appropriate than app.all('/api/{*splat}', auth)?
followUps.purpose: Probe app.use versus app.all design choice.

followUps.question: What happens if an app.all precondition never calls next and never sends a response?

en.title: app.all for all methods
en.displayIntent: Check understanding of all-method route matching and path-specific preconditions.
guidance: Define app.all as matching every HTTP method on the specified path.
Use authentication or resource loading as examples.
Explain that callbacks can call next to continue matching later routes.
Contrast app.all with app.use at a practical level.
commonMistakes: Thinking app.all means every path.
Using app.all instead of clear method-specific routes.
Forgetting next() when app.all is used as middleware.
Not recognizing order and broad-match risks.

vi.title: app.all cho mọi HTTP method
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về route match mọi method và precondition theo path.
guidance: Định nghĩa app.all là match mọi HTTP method trên path được chỉ định.
Dùng authentication hoặc load resource làm ví dụ.
Giải thích callback có thể gọi next để tiếp tục match route phía sau.
So sánh app.all với app.use ở mức thực tế.
commonMistakes: Tưởng app.all nghĩa là mọi path.
Dùng app.all thay cho route theo method rõ ràng.
Quên next() khi dùng app.all như middleware.
Không nhận ra rủi ro về thứ tự và match quá rộng.

ja.title: すべての method に対する app.all
ja.displayQuestion: app.all() は何に使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: 全 HTTP method に対する route matching と path-specific precondition の理解を確認する。
guidance: app.all は指定 path に対してすべての HTTP method に match すると定義する。
authentication や resource loading を例に使う。
callback は next を呼んで後続 route に処理を続けられることを説明する。
app.all と app.use を実務的に比較する。
commonMistakes: app.all がすべての path という意味だと思う。
明確な method-specific route の代わりに app.all を多用する。
app.all を middleware として使う時に next() を忘れる。
順序や広すぎる match のリスクを認識しない。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Route methods
sourceReferences.note: Used for app.all as the special routing method for all HTTP methods.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: app.all
sourceReferences.note: Used for all-method path-specific precondition examples.

intent: Check whether the candidate can chain multiple handlers on a single route and reason about next, arrays, preconditions, and final response handling.
primaryQuestion: How do you chain multiple handlers into one Express route?

expectedSignals.label: Shows route-level middleware with app.get('/path', mw1, mw2, handler) or router.get('/path', [mw1, mw2], handler).
Explains that each non-final handler should call next() or pass an error with next(err).
Mentions that arrays and combinations of arrays and functions are valid callback formats.
Uses handler chains for validation, authentication, authorization, loading resources, and then the final controller.
Mentions next('route') as an escape hatch for skipping remaining callbacks in the current route when conditional routing is needed.

redFlags: Writes multiple handlers but none call next, causing the request to hang.
Calls next after final res.json without understanding duplicate response risk.
Puts business logic into many opaque middleware functions without clear responsibility.
Cannot distinguish route-level handler chaining from app.route or router.route method chaining.

scoringHints.description: Demonstrates multiple callback forms, explains next and error flow, gives realistic precondition examples, and knows next('route') nuance.
Can chain handlers and use next but misses arrays, next('route'), or final response nuance.
Understands middleware vaguely but cannot implement a reliable route chain.
Answer would likely hang requests or send duplicate responses.

followUps.question: Can you chain validateUserId, requireAuth, and getUserHandler for GET /users/:id?
followUps.purpose: Make the candidate show a real route chain.

followUps.question: What happens if validateUserId neither sends a 400 nor calls next()?
When would next('route') be useful instead of next()?

en.title: Chaining route handlers
en.displayQuestion: How do you chain multiple handlers into one Express route?
en.displayIntent: Check route-level middleware composition and next() control flow.
guidance: Show app.get('/users/:id', validate, auth, controller).
Mention arrays and mixed arrays/functions as valid callback forms.
Explain next(), next(err), and final response responsibilities.
Use validation and authentication as practical reasons for chaining.
commonMistakes: Forgetting next() in a precondition handler.
Calling next after sending the final response.
Creating many tiny middleware functions with unclear responsibilities.
Confusing handler chaining with app.route().get().post() method chaining.

vi.title: Chain nhiều route handler
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để chain nhiều handler vào một route?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cách compose middleware ở cấp route và control flow qua next().
guidance: Minh họa app.get('/users/:id', validate, auth, controller).
Nhắc rằng array và kết hợp array/function đều là callback form hợp lệ.
Giải thích trách nhiệm của next(), next(err) và final response.
Dùng validation và authentication như lý do thực tế để chain handler.
commonMistakes: Quên next() trong handler precondition.
Gọi next sau khi đã gửi final response.
Tạo quá nhiều middleware nhỏ nhưng trách nhiệm không rõ.
Nhầm handler chaining với method chaining app.route().get().post().

ja.title: route handler の chaining
ja.displayQuestion: 1 つの Express route に複数の handler を chain するにはどうしますか。
ja.displayIntent: route-level middleware composition と next() control flow を確認する。
guidance: app.get('/users/:id', validate, auth, controller) を例に示す。
array と array/function の組み合わせも有効な callback form だと述べる。
next()、next(err)、final response の責務を説明する。
validation と authentication を chaining の実務的な理由として使う。
commonMistakes: precondition handler で next() を忘れる。
final response を送った後に next を呼ぶ。
責務が不明な小さすぎる middleware を大量に作る。
handler chaining と app.route().get().post() の method chaining を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Route handlers
sourceReferences.note: Used for multiple callbacks, arrays, combinations, and next('route').

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware arrays and request-response control flow.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: app.METHOD
sourceReferences.note: Used for accepted callback formats and promise rejection forwarding.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can distinguish application-level middleware from router-level middleware and reason about mount paths, scope, and execution order.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between router.use() and app.use() in the context of Express Router?

expectedSignals.label: Explains app.use mounts middleware or routers at the application level, affecting requests that match the app-level mount path.
Explains router.use mounts middleware within a specific router, affecting requests that are passed into that router and match the router-level path.
Understands that when a router is mounted, its internal paths are relative to the mount path.
Mentions that the mount path is stripped from req.url inside mounted middleware, while req.originalUrl and req.baseUrl can help understand the full request context.
Uses router.use for feature-scoped middleware such as auth for users routes, and app.use for global middleware such as JSON parsing, request logging, CORS, or mounting the router itself.
Mentions order still matters both at app level and inside the router.

redFlags: Says router.use and app.use are identical with no scope difference.
Registers feature-specific middleware globally without recognizing side effects.
Cannot predict which paths router.use('/admin', mw) handles after app.use('/api', router).
Ignores mount path stripping and gets confused by req.url inside nested routers.

scoringHints.description: Explains app-level versus router-level scope, mount path composition, stripped paths, request URL properties, practical use cases, and ordering.
Understands scope difference but misses mount path stripping or URL property nuance.
Can use router.use but cannot predict behavior in nested or prefixed routers.
Answer shows incorrect middleware scoping.

followUps.question: If app.use('/api', router) and router.use('/users', mw), which full path prefix triggers mw?
Which middleware belongs at app level: express.json, CORS, requireAdminForUsersRouter, and a global request logger?
Why might req.url look different inside a mounted router than the original client URL?

en.title: router.use versus app.use
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between router.use() and app.use() in the context of Express Router?
en.displayIntent: Check middleware scope, mount path composition, and router-level ordering.
guidance: Explain app-level mounting versus router-level mounting.
Describe how app.use('/api', router) composes with router.use('/users', middleware).
Mention req.baseUrl, req.originalUrl, and mount path stripping if the candidate is mid-level or above.
Give examples of global middleware and feature-scoped middleware.
commonMistakes: Treating router.use and app.use as the same scope.
Making feature-specific middleware global.
Forgetting that router paths are relative to where the router is mounted.
Being surprised by req.url inside mounted middleware.

vi.title: router.use và app.use
vi.displayQuestion: Sự khác biệt giữa router.use() và app.use() trong context của Router?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra scope middleware, cách ghép mount path và thứ tự trong router.
guidance: Giải thích app-level mounting và router-level mounting.
Mô tả app.use('/api', router) ghép với router.use('/users', middleware) như thế nào.
Nhắc req.baseUrl, req.originalUrl và việc strip mount path nếu ứng viên ở mid trở lên.
Đưa ví dụ middleware global và middleware theo feature.
commonMistakes: Xem router.use và app.use là cùng scope.
Đưa middleware riêng của feature lên global.
Quên path trong router là tương đối với nơi router được mount.
Bất ngờ khi req.url bên trong mounted middleware khác URL gốc.

ja.title: router.use と app.use
ja.displayQuestion: Express Router の context で router.use() と app.use() はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: middleware scope、mount path composition、router-level ordering を確認する。
guidance: app-level mounting と router-level mounting を説明する。
app.use('/api', router) と router.use('/users', middleware) がどう合成されるか説明する。
mid-level 以上なら req.baseUrl、req.originalUrl、mount path stripping に触れる。
global middleware と feature-scoped middleware の例を出す。
commonMistakes: router.use と app.use を同じ scope として扱う。
feature-specific middleware を global にしてしまう。
router 内の path が mount 位置に対する相対 path であることを忘れる。
mounted middleware 内の req.url が元 URL と違うことに驚く。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for application-level and router-level middleware.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: router.use
sourceReferences.note: Used for router.use behavior, mount path stripping, and ordering.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: req.baseUrl
sourceReferences.note: Used for mounted router base URL behavior.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can pass per-request data safely between middleware and handlers without global mutable state or unclear contracts.
primaryQuestion: How do you pass data from one middleware to the next middleware in Express?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that middleware can attach per-request data to req, such as req.user, req.requestId, or req.validatedBody.
Mentions res.locals as another per-request place for data intended for downstream response handling or templates.
Calls next() after attaching data, or next(err) if the data cannot be produced safely.
Avoids global variables or module-level mutable state for request-specific data.
In TypeScript, mentions augmenting the Express Request type or using a typed request/context interface instead of relying on any.
Recognizes that data attached to req or res.locals should have clear ownership, naming, validation, and cleanup expectations.

redFlags: Uses global variables to store the current user or current request data.
Mutates req with ambiguous property names that collide with framework or middleware fields.
Forgets to call next after setting the data.
Assumes data attached by one router is available to unrelated routers regardless of order and path matching.

scoringHints.description: Explains req and res.locals, per-request lifetime, next/error flow, TypeScript typing, and avoids global mutable state.
Knows to attach data to req but misses res.locals, typing, or ownership concerns.
Can hack data into a handler but lacks a safe per-request data model.
Answer suggests unsafe shared state or unreliable middleware flow.

followUps.question: Why is a module-level currentUser variable unsafe in an Express server?
Where would you store an authenticated user for downstream handlers, and how would you type it in TypeScript?
followUps.purpose: Check practical Express plus TypeScript approach.

followUps.question: What is the difference between putting data on req.user and res.locals.user?
followUps.purpose: Probe request context versus response-local usage.

en.title: Passing data between middleware
en.displayQuestion: How do you pass data from one middleware to the next middleware in Express?
en.displayIntent: Check safe per-request context sharing through req or res.locals.
guidance: Explain attaching data to req for request context such as req.user.
Mention res.locals for response-local data used downstream.
Explain that the middleware must call next or next(err).
Warn against global mutable state and mention TypeScript request augmentation when relevant.
commonMistakes: Using global variables for request-specific data.
Adding unclear fields to req with no typing or ownership.
Forgetting next after setting data.
Assuming middleware always ran before a handler without checking order and path.

vi.title: Truyền dữ liệu giữa middleware
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để truyền dữ liệu từ middleware này sang middleware tiếp theo?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cách chia sẻ context theo từng request qua req hoặc res.locals một cách an toàn.
guidance: Giải thích việc gắn dữ liệu vào req cho request context như req.user.
Nhắc res.locals cho dữ liệu response-local dùng ở downstream.
Giải thích middleware phải gọi next hoặc next(err).
Cảnh báo không dùng global mutable state và nhắc augment Request type trong TypeScript khi phù hợp.
commonMistakes: Dùng global variable cho dữ liệu riêng của từng request.
Gắn field mơ hồ vào req mà không có typing hoặc ownership.
Quên next sau khi set dữ liệu.
Giả định middleware luôn chạy trước handler mà không kiểm tra order và path.

ja.title: middleware 間の data 受け渡し
ja.displayQuestion: Express である middleware から次の middleware に data を渡すにはどうしますか。
ja.displayIntent: req または res.locals による安全な per-request context sharing を確認する。
guidance: req.user のように request context を req に付与する方法を説明する。
downstream の response 処理で使う data には res.locals も使えることを述べる。
middleware は next または next(err) を呼ぶ必要があると説明する。
global mutable state を避け、必要なら TypeScript の Request augmentation に触れる。
commonMistakes: request-specific data に global variable を使う。
typing や ownership なしに曖昧な field を req に追加する。
data を設定した後に next を忘れる。
order と path を確認せず middleware が必ず先に走ったと思い込む。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware access to req, res, next, and ability to modify request/response objects.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: res.locals
sourceReferences.note: Used for response-local variables available during the request-response cycle.

sourceReferences.label: Express 5.x API: router.param
sourceReferences.note: Used for examples of preloading route parameter data onto req.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can organize Express routes in a larger project using pragmatic MVC or feature-module boundaries without overloading route handlers.
primaryQuestion: How would you organize routes in a large Express project following an MVC-style structure?

expectedSignals.label: Separates route definitions from controllers, service/business logic, data access, validation, middleware, and app bootstrap.
Uses feature routers such as users.routes.ts mounted from a central api router or app.ts with prefixes and versions.
Keeps controllers thin: parse request data, call services, shape responses, and delegate business decisions to services or use cases.
Keeps database access in repositories/models or data access modules rather than directly in route files.
Centralizes cross-cutting concerns such as auth, validation, error handling, logging, configuration, and request IDs.
Chooses abstractions pragmatically: avoids both one giant app file and unnecessary framework-heavy layers for small features.
Mentions tests for controllers/routes and services, plus consistent error and response contracts.

redFlags: Puts all business logic, SQL, validation, and response shaping inside route handlers.
Creates folders named routes/controllers/services but responsibilities remain mixed.
Adds too many abstractions without a concrete maintainability problem.
Uses global mutable singletons everywhere and makes tests order-dependent.
Cannot explain where validation, authorization, and error handling belong.

scoringHints.description: Presents a pragmatic MVC or feature-module layout with clear responsibilities, dependency boundaries, cross-cutting middleware, testing, and trade-offs.
Has a reasonable folder structure but misses one major boundary such as validation, data access, or centralized errors.
Lists folders but cannot explain responsibilities or how requests flow through them.
Answer would lead to a difficult-to-maintain Express codebase.

followUps.question: Walk me through GET /api/v1/users/:id from router to controller to service to repository and back to the response.
followUps.purpose: Force a concrete request flow through the proposed structure.

followUps.question: When would you keep simple logic in a controller instead of creating another service layer?
Where would validation, authorization, and centralized error handling live in your structure?

en.title: Organizing large Express routes
en.displayQuestion: How would you organize routes in a large Express project following an MVC-style structure?
en.displayIntent: Check maintainable Express architecture, route ownership, and separation of concerns.
guidance: Describe the request flow through router, controller, service, repository/model, and error handler.
Explain which responsibility belongs in each layer and which should not.
Use feature routers and versioned API prefixes for route organization.
Discuss validation, auth, logging, configuration, tests, and consistent errors as cross-cutting concerns.
commonMistakes: Putting business logic and database calls directly in route handlers.
Creating MVC folders without real responsibility boundaries.
Overengineering simple features with unnecessary layers.
Ignoring centralized validation, authorization, errors, and observability.

vi.title: Tổ chức routes Express trong dự án lớn
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để tổ chức routes trong dự án Express lớn theo chuẩn MVC?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra kiến trúc Express dễ maintain, ownership của route và separation of concerns.
guidance: Mô tả request flow qua router, controller, service, repository/model và error handler.
Giải thích trách nhiệm thuộc về từng layer và điều gì không nên đặt ở layer đó.
Dùng feature router và API prefix có version để tổ chức route.
Nói về validation, auth, logging, configuration, test và error nhất quán như cross-cutting concerns.
commonMistakes: Đưa business logic và database call trực tiếp vào route handler.
Tạo folder MVC nhưng không có boundary trách nhiệm thật.
Overengineer feature đơn giản với quá nhiều layer không cần thiết.
Bỏ qua centralized validation, authorization, error và observability.

ja.title: 大規模 Express routes の構成
ja.displayQuestion: 大きな Express project で MVC-style に routes を構成するにはどうしますか。
ja.displayIntent: maintainable な Express architecture、route ownership、separation of concerns を確認する。
guidance: request flow を router、controller、service、repository/model、error handler の順に説明する。
各 layer の責務と、そこに置くべきでないものを説明する。
feature router と versioned API prefix で route を整理する。
validation、auth、logging、configuration、tests、一貫した errors を cross-cutting concerns として説明する。
commonMistakes: business logic と database call を route handler に直接入れる。
MVC folder は作るが実際の責務 boundary がない。
単純な feature に不要な layer を足しすぎる。
centralized validation、authorization、errors、observability を無視する。

sourceReferences.note: Used for modular routers and mountable route handlers.

sourceReferences.note: Used for global and router-level middleware organization.
